fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CAREW J:

This matter was listed for a five-day trial commencing on 1 December 2025 in relation to a parenting and property dispute between the applicant, Ms Sauter (“the wife”), and the first respondent, Mr Holt (“the husband”). The property dispute also includes a second respondent, Ms Gajos (“the third party”), from whom the husband (either through entities controlled by him or in a personal capacity) borrowed money and to whom the husband transferred control of three [redacted] businesses after separation, purportedly in satisfaction of loans.
The relief sought by the wife substantially changed during the trial. For example, the wife no longer seeks to set aside various loan agreements between the entities controlled by the husband, the husband as guarantor and the third party and accepts that loans by the third party were made and made on the dates alleged.
On the first day of trial, a parenting order was made by consent. The trial continued in relation to the property dispute only, with the wife also abandoning her claim for spouse maintenance.
For reasons to be explained, the transfers of shares in companies controlled by the husband to the third party will be set aside. The third party will be repaid the balance outstanding on her loans with interest up to 25 May 2023. The wife will retain a real property. All remaining real properties and the [redacted] businesses will be sold with the overall property distribution to be divided between the wife and the husband in the proportion 65/35 in the wife’s favour.

LEGAL FEES

The wife’s costs until the end of the hearing were estimated to be $912,528.52 (there is also mention of a paid legal fee of $119,390.32 in the wife’s Costs Notice filed on 29 November 2025, which may be in addition to this figure).
The husband’s costs until the end of the hearing were estimated to be $414,321.06.
The third party’s costs until the end of the hearing were estimated to be $135,705.30.

ISSUES

When this matter was set down for trial the parties identified the following very broad, and largely unhelpful, list of issues for my determination:
(1) Identification and value of asset pool;

(2) Whether the loan agreements are transactions within the meaning of s 106B of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”);

(3) The 106B hearing and consequential orders including whether the third party seeks any claim;

(4) Financial and non-financial contribution of the wife and husband; and

(5) The wife and husband’s property adjustment.

Notwithstanding being invited to confer and redefine the real issues requiring determination, counsel for the parties failed to do so. Accordingly, I have identified the following issues for determination taking into account the numerous concessions made during the trial:
(1) Were the transfers of shares in D Pty Ltd (“D Pty Ltd”) and E Pty Ltd (“E Pty Ltd”) by the husband to the third party made, or proposed to be made, to defeat an anticipated order in the property settlement proceedings or, irrespective of intention, were they likely to defeat any such order?

(2) At the time of the share transfers, did the value of the [redacted] businesses conducted by D Pty Ltd and E Pty Ltd ‘roughly equate’ to the balance of the loans (together with interest) owing to the third party?

(3) Is there sufficient other property to achieve a just and equitable settlement without setting aside the share transfers?

(4) Should the loans be treated as a liability of the husband alone and not brought into account in the balance sheet?

(5) If the share transfers are set aside, should Business F operated by D Pty Ltd and Business G and Business H operated by E Pty Ltd be sold to the third party (as proposed by the husband) or put on the open market for sale?

(6) Taking into account the myriad of contributions made by each of the husband and the wife to the property of the parties to the marriage or either of them including any effect on the wife of the admitted family violence perpetrated upon her by the husband, and the considerations relating to current and future circumstances of the husband and the wife, in what proportion should the net property of the husband and wife or either of them be divided?

WHAT IS EACH PARTY SEEKING?

On the fifth day of the trial, the wife was granted leave to further amend the orders sought by her (without objection by the other parties), and the orders she seeks, including in the alternative, are set out in Exhibit 29 (although further amendments were made during submissions without objection and are marked up on the face of the Exhibit). The wife seeks, among other things, declarations that the three loans advanced by the third party to E Pty Ltd (loans advanced on 9 September 2019 and 5 October 2022) and D Pty Ltd (loan advanced on 8 April 2020) guaranteed by the husband, were in fact loans to the husband personally and not to the companies. Curiously, only if those declarations are not made does the wife seek to set aside the share transfers by the husband to the third party in E Pty Ltd and D Pty Ltd. The wife nevertheless seeks to include as property of the parties to the marriage or either of them the “net value of the businesses” conducted by D Pty Ltd and E Pty Ltd and a division in the proportion 60/40 in her favour. As part of her property settlement, the wife seeks to retain the home situated at EE Circuit, Suburb FF (“the Suburb FF property”), and otherwise for all other real property, and the [redacted] businesses operated by D Pty Ltd and E Pty Ltd, to be sold.
On the fifth day of trial, the husband was also granted leave (without objection) to further amend the orders sought by him, which are set out, in the alternative, in Exhibit 31 (although further amendments were made during submissions, without objection and are marked up on the face of the Exhibit). The husband seeks that all real property be sold, including the Suburb FF property (although during submissions, counsel for the husband submitted that there was “no opposition for [the wife] to keep it”), and that the net proceeds of sale be divided in the proportion 60/40 in his favour and that he and the wife otherwise retain all their other property and superannuation. The alternative orders sought by the husband (first raised on the last day of trial) are for the third party to buy the “[redacted] businesses” for $1,171,800 and that that sum also be divided between himself and the wife in the proportion 60/40 in his favour. In the event the share transfers are set aside, the husband opposes any repayment of the loan balances submitting, they should be “ignored”. No submissions were made in support of this contention.
The effect of the relief sought by the third party is to dismiss the application to set aside the share transfers or in the alternative, the repayment of the loans together with interest until the date of payment. The orders sought by the third party including in the alternative are set out in Exhibit 30 (although I was unable to make any sense of the reference to paragraph numbers in paragraph 3 of the Exhibit).

BACKGROUND

The wife and the husband commenced cohabitation in or about October 2014, married in B Country in 2016 and separated on 28 December 2022, according to the wife, or January 2022, according to the husband (although it was initially conceded by counsel for the husband that separation occurred in early January 2023). I note that the third party pleads in her Points of Defence that the husband told her on or around 30 March 2023 that he and the wife had separated on 28 December 2022. The husband did not dispute this. No party sought a finding as to the actual date of separation nor was it submitted that it would make a material difference to the outcome.
There are two children of the relationship, X (otherwise known as [redacted]), born on [redacted] 2018 and Y (otherwise known as [redacted]), born on [redacted] 2020. Pursuant to the consent order made on the first day of trial, the children will continue to live with the mother and spend alternate weekends and holiday time with the father.
It is common ground that the wife moved with the children from City K to Brisbane in September 2019 and lived in Brisbane with the children until July 2020 (although neither the husband nor the wife contend that this was a period of formal separation). It is also common ground that the wife and children left Australia on 28 January 2022 and lived in Country B until December 2022, when she and the children returned to Australia. The wife disputes the husband’s characterisation of this period as a formal separation.
D Pty Ltd was incorporated on 5 March 2018, and the husband was its sole director until 25 May 2023 when he resigned as director and appointed the third party as director. The husband held all 10 fully paid ordinary shares in D Pty Ltd from its incorporation until 25 May 2023 when he transferred his shares to the third party. D Pty Ltd purchased the business known as Business F in around April 2018 and continues to operate it.
E Pty Ltd was incorporated on 3 July 2019, and the husband was its sole director until 25 May 2023 when he resigned as director and appointed the third party as director. The husband held all 10 fully paid ordinary shares in E Pty Ltd from its incorporation until he transferred five shares to the third party on 25 May 2023 and the remaining five shares on 5 June 2023. E Pty Ltd commenced the business known as Business G in or about February 2020 and continues to operate it. E Pty Ltd commenced the business known as Business H in or about June 2022 and continues to operate it.
The wife deposes in her trial affidavit that when E Pty Ltd was established she “was the visa applicant and was prohibited from being involved with any other business” which is “why [the husband] was appointed as a director and shareholder with a 100% share”. Notwithstanding the apparent conditions relating to her visa, the wife deposes that she and the husband opened the two businesses known as Business G and Business H.
It is now common ground that the husband received cash from the third party on 9 September 2019, 8 April 2020 and 5 October 2022 (“the loans”) totalling $650,000. The husband contends that he used the loans for renovations and running costs of the [redacted] businesses. It is common ground that at the time the husband transferred the shares in D Pty Ltd and E Pty Ltd to the third party, only $160,000 of the principal had been repaid and no interest.
In 2021, the husband and wife jointly purchased vacant land at EE Circuit, Suburb FF (“the Suburb FF property”) for $382,200 after jointly borrowing $304,571.42 from the National Australia Bank (“NAB”). A home was constructed on the property in 2022 for $371,824 utilising a further loan of $305,760 from NAB. The wife and children live in the Suburb FF property.
In 2022, the husband purchased vacant land in his sole name at Q Street, Suburb R (“the Suburb R property”) for $690,000 after borrowing $549,701.81 from NAB. A home was constructed in 2022 for $695,170 utilising a further loan of $477,519.20 from NAB. The husband lives in the Suburb R property.
In June 2022, the husband purchased a property at U Avenue, Suburb V (“the Suburb V property”) for $410,000 after borrowing $326,540.81 from NAB. This property is rented out for $2,200 per month. The property is apparently registered in the name of SS Pty Ltd as trustee for the TT Trust. The wife and husband have treated this property as the property of the husband presumably because the trustee and the trust are alter egos of the husband.
In November 2022, the husband purchased a property in his sole name at AA Street, Suburb BB (“the Suburb BB property”) for $745,000 after borrowing $596,000 from NAB. The Suburb BB property comprises three units. Employees of the [redacted] businesses live at this property and the rent for units 1 and 3 is paid by the third party. Rent for units 1 and 3 is $3,200 per month and rent for unit 2 is $2,150 per month.
The husband contends that the balance purchase price required for the acquisition of the various properties and the balance construction costs for the homes built on the Suburb FF property and the Suburb R property were paid by him from his salary received from the [redacted] businesses. The wife concedes that some money saved by them from wages was used for the purchase of the Suburb R property, but only in relation to the land. However, the report by the single expert accountant, Mr JJ, filed on 24 November 2025 (“the 2025 report”) identifies that the husband used $228,226 from D Pty Ltd on the Suburb R property “(being deposit to the construction company; construction of a swimming pool; fees for council approval; appliances for the property; associated legal fees and the like)” (emphasis removed). The single expert has treated these funds as a loan to the husband. Additionally, the 2025 report identifies additional director’s loans to the husband of $103,111, being $60,852 in the 2019 financial year and $42,259 in the 2020 financial year. Prior to their separation, the wife deposes in her trial affidavit that the husband “brought cash from the business occasionally and saved the money in his personal safe”. The wife further contends that she “saw substantial amounts”. The wife also alleges that the husband used cash to pay for the stamp duty incurred on the purchase of the real properties. The husband conceded during his oral evidence to keeping sums of cash in his home safe. It is common ground that the husband also had two safes at the [redacted] business premises in City K.
The wife commenced court proceedings on 29 January 2023.
On 6 March 2023, the husband withdrew $100,000 from the D Pty Ltd bank account used to operate Business F. On this date, the husband also established the Holt Unit Trust with himself as the sole unit holder. The husband admits in his Points of Defence (to the third party’s Points of Cross Claim) the third party’s assertion that the $100,000 was applied towards “legal fees”. No submissions were made in relation to this sum.
On 7 March 2023, the husband withdrew $228,225.56 from the same bank account which was applied to the Suburb R property as described above.
On or about 30 March 2023, the husband told the third party that the wife was seeking a property settlement. The third party then made a demand for repayment of the loans.
On 30 March 2023, the husband withdrew $300,000 from his personal bank account and initially paid the entire sum to the third party in cash but, upon his request, the third party returned $140,000 to the husband. It is common ground that the $160,000 retained by the third party was a repayment of principal against the first loan.
The husband contends in his trial affidavit that his calculation of the balance owing to the third party on the three loans in or about June 2023 was “around $665,000” including interest.
The third party contends in her trial affidavit that in mid-May 2023 the balance owing on the first and second loans was $662,169.47, including interest.
The husband concedes that in his financial statement filed on 7 March 2023, he estimated that the [redacted] businesses were worth $1,800,000 and he contends that he updated his estimated value of the [redacted] businesses on 21 May 2023 to $1,200,000.
In April 2023, the husband purchased a property at S Street, Suburb T (“the Suburb T property”), for $530,000 after borrowing $424,000 from NAB. This property is rented out for $3,120 per month. The property is apparently registered in the name of the Holt Unit Pty Ltd as trustee for the Holt Unit Trust. The wife and husband have treated this property as the property of the husband presumably because the trustee and the trust are alter egos of the husband.
On 19 May 2023, the husband offered to transfer his share in the [redacted] businesses to the third party purportedly in satisfaction of the outstanding loans and interest as provided for in the loan agreements.
On 25 May 2023, the husband transferred 100 per cent of his shares in D Pty Ltd and 50 per cent of his shares in E Pty Ltd to the third party purportedly in part satisfaction of the outstanding loans and interest as provided for in the loan agreements.
On 5 June 2023, the husband transferred his remaining 50 per cent share in E Pty Ltd to the third party purportedly in satisfaction of the balance of outstanding loans and interest as provided for in the loan agreements.
As the loans made by the third party to D Pty Ltd and E Pty Ltd and guaranteed by the husband assumed considerable focus during the trial, it will be helpful to set out the terms of each loan.

THE LOANS

The first loan – 9 September 2019 – Exhibit 26

The first loan was advanced pursuant to a loan agreement between the third party as the lender, E Pty Ltd as the borrower and with the husband as guarantor. The agreement was signed by the third party and by the husband as director of E Pty Ltd and in his personal capacity on 9 September 2019. In a schedule to the loan agreement, the “terms of the loan agreement”[1] were set out as follows:
Approved Purpose	Fitout costs for the 2nd store in [City W].
Monies (Loan Amount)	$200,000
Interest Rate	15% per Annum
Repayment Terms	The Borrower shall pay to the Lender the Interest, Other Amounts and Monies in accordance with the following terms:

Termination Date: The date which is twenty four (24) months from the date of the initial advance.
Interest Payment: Upfront Payment
Repayment: The Borrower must pay to the Lender the balance, if any, of the Debt on the Termination Date.
Early Repayment: Not Applicable.
The Borrower to repay to the Lender on demand any moneys, which the Lender may reasonably see fit to pay to remedy any default of the Borrower.
In the event that the Borrower defaults in satisfying the obligations stipulated in this Agreement, the Lender is expressly authorized to acquire equity interest of the Borrower and to assume control over the business operations thereof.

Commencement date	9th September 2019
State	QLD
(Emphasis in original)
Under the loan agreement, the lender, namely, the third party, agreed to do certain things including the following:
2.1 ...
(b) INTEREST: The Lender will charge the Interest on the Monies, or such of the Monies as shall be unpaid, on each Interest Period, until repaid in full. If the Borrower fails to pay Interest on or before a Due Date, then the Lender may at any time during the Loan Term, without prejudice to its other rights and remedies, add to the Monies any part of the Interest which is not paid on the Due Date;
(Emphasis in original)
The borrower, namely, E Pty Ltd, made certain promises including the following:
3.1 ...
(b) INTEREST: The Borrower will pay Interest on the Monies, or such of the Monies as shall be unpaid, on each Interest Period, until repaid in full;
(Emphasis in original)
The guarantor, namely the husband, guaranteed the borrower’s promise.
Under the definitions and interpretation section of the agreement is the following:
10.1 ...

“Interest Period” means each month of the loan, or part thereof. Interest shall compound if not paid each Interest Period, and be added to the Monies. Unless written consent is given by the Lender to the Borrower and/or Guarantor, the Interest shall be payable on each Interest Period to the Lender. A failure to do so constitutes a Default of the Agreement by the Borrower and/or Guarantor.

(Emphasis in original)
The third party advanced the sum of $200,000 in cash to the husband on 9 September 2019.
The husband contends that the $200,000 cash loan was used for the purpose identified in the loan agreement, namely, fitout costs for Business G (the second business in City W), particularised (in general terms) in his trial affidavit as follows:
(a) Building materials $36,000

(b) Signage $10,200

(c) Labour $30,200

(d) Supervising $18,400

(e) Margin $23,400

(f) Expense $19,800

(g) Timber $29,000

(h) Sundry $16,700

(i) Miscellaneous $16,300

Total: $200,000

Elsewhere in his trial affidavit the husband gives a different account of how he utilised the first loan as follows:
(a) “Approximately $45,000 for Tiling and stone works paid to [Mr HH]”;

(b) “Approximately $35,000 for Air-conditioning paid to [Business UU]”;

(c) “Approximately $35[,]000 for shutter and auto-door installation paid to [Business VV]”;

(d) “Approximately $40,000 for shelf and stainless steel structure works paid to [Business WW]”;

(e) “Approximately $25,000 for Signage paid to unknown trader engaged by [Business AAA]”; and

(f) “Approximately $5,000-$10,000 for other expenses such as cleaning and waste disposal”.

Total: $185,000 - $190,000

The wife’s reply affidavit identifies that she either denies or does not admit the use of the first loan received by the husband.
The only evidence relied upon by the husband to corroborate how he used the first loan is an affidavit from Mr HH, a tiler, who deposes that he was paid an initial cash sum of about $20,000 for material costs and a labour fee of about $25,000 also in cash. Neither payment included GST. The wife concedes the payment of $45,000 in cash to this person.
The husband provided no explanation for his failure to call evidence from the other subcontractors to whom he contends he paid cash sums. The husband contends that he did not receive any invoices from the subcontractors nor did he obtain receipts for any of the cash payments allegedly made to the subcontractors.
No interest payments were made to the third party during the term of the loan, and the principal was not repaid on the due date, namely, 9 September 2021. No action was taken by the third party to acquire any equity in E Pty Ltd at that time.
On or about 30 March 2023, the third party demanded repayment of the loan after the husband told her that the wife had commenced proceedings for property settlement. The husband initially paid $300,000 in cash to the third party but $140,000 was returned to him by the third party upon his request. The principal then owing to the third party on the first loan was $40,000. No interest had been paid on the loan prior to the husband transferring his shares in E Pty Ltd to the third party.

The second loan – 8 April 2020 – Exhibit 27

The second loan was advanced pursuant to a loan agreement between the third party as the lender, D Pty Ltd as the borrower, and the husband as guarantor. The agreement was signed by the third party and the husband as director of D Pty Ltd and in his personal capacity on 8 April 2020. In a schedule to the loan agreement, the “terms of the loan agreement” were set out as follows:

Monies (Loan Amount)	$200,000

Repayment Terms	The Borrower shall pay to the Lender the Interest, Other Amounts and Monies in accordance with the following terms:

In the event that the Borrower defaults in satisfying the obligations stipulated in this Agreement, the Lender is expressly authorized to acquire equity interest of the Borrower and to assume control over the business operations thereof.

Commencement Date	8th of April 2020

2.1 ...
(b) INTEREST: The Lender will charge the Interest on the Monies, or such of the Monies as shall be unpaid, on each Interest Period, until repaid in full. If the Borrower fails to pay Interest on or before a Due Date, then the Lender may at any time during the Loan Term, without prejudice to its other rights and remedies, add to the Monies any part of the Interest which is not paid on the Due Date;
(Emphasis in original)
The borrower, namely, E Pty Ltd, made certain promises including the following:

(b) INTEREST: The Borrower will pay Interest on the Monies, or such of the Monies as shall be unpaid, on each Interest Period, until repaid in full;
(Emphasis in original)

A failure to do so constitutes a Default of the Agreement by the Borrower and/or Guarantor.

(Emphasis in original)
The third party advanced the sum of $200,000 in cash to the husband on 8 April 2020.
The husband deposes in his trial affidavit that he kept the cash in his safe at home and used the cash to pay outstanding invoices issued by Business BBB totalling $220,438.20 for the period 4 March 2020 to 29 April 2020. The invoices were not tendered.
During cross-examination, the husband said that he used most of the cash to pay “[Business BBB] main supplier, and also equipment and miscellaneous items for the business” but also paid some staff in cash. The husband said he paid Business BBB “about 150 to 160 or 170K”. The husband did not produce any receipts for any payments made to Business BBB. The husband did not produce any evidence that he used part of the cash loan to pay staff.
No repayments were made on this loan, and no interest had been paid prior to the husband transferring his shares in D Pty Ltd to the third party.

The third loan agreement – 5 October 2022 – Exhibit 28

The third loan was advanced pursuant to a loan agreement between the third party as the lender, E Pty Ltd as the borrower, and the husband as guarantor. The agreement was signed by the third party and the husband as director of E Pty Ltd and in his personal capacity on 5 October 2022. The terms of the loan agreement were set out in a schedule to the agreement as follows:
Approved Purpose	Costs associated with New [Business]
Monies (Loan Amount)	$250,000
Interest Rate	18% Per Annum

The third party advanced the sum of $150,000 in cash to the husband on 3 April 2022 and $100,000 in cash to the husband on 5 October 2022 (although the only receipt which is Exhibit 22 is dated 5 October 2022 and refers to the total loan of $250,000). Under the terms of the agreement, the principal was not required to be paid until 3 April 2024 at the earliest.
The husband contends that he used $187,235.40 for “Interior Fit-out” for the third business set up at Suburb V on an “[u]nknown” date and $62,764.60 “for others” paid on “[v]arious dates from 5 October 2022”. Elsewhere in his trial affidavit the husband deposes that he used the first $150,000 cash to pay the following cash payments:
(a) “Approximately $40,000 for tiling and stone works, paid to the unknown carpenters engaged by [Business AAA]”;

(b) “Approximately $50,000 for carpentry works paid to unknown carpenter engaged by [Business AAA]”;

(c) “Approximately $20,000 for shelf and stainless steel structure works paid to [Business WW]”;

(d) “Approximately $30,000 for air-conditioning works paid to [Business CCC]”;

(e) “Approximately $10,000 for the purchase of miscellaneous items”.

Total: $150,000

As to the second tranche of $100,000 cash, the husband deposes to using the funds as follows:
(a) “Approximately $20,000 for signage works, paid to unknown trader engaged by [Business AAA]”;

(b) “Approximately $20,000 for shutter and auto-door installation paid to the [Business DDD]”; and

(c) “Approximately $60,000 for the purchase of furniture, tools, initial trading stocks and other miscellaneous items”.

Total: $100,000

No interest payments were made on this loan prior to the husband transferring his shares in E Pty Ltd to the third party.

SECTION 106B CLAIM

The wife seeks to set aside the transfer of shares in D Pty Ltd and E Pty Ltd by the husband to the third party. The husband and third party resist the relief sought by the wife.
Section 106B of the Act relevantly provides:
106B Transactions to defeat claims
(1) In proceedings under this Act, the court may set aside ... the making of an instrument or disposition by ... a party, which is made ...to defeat an ... anticipated order in those proceedings or which, irrespective of intention, is likely to defeat any such order.
...
(3) The court must have regard to the interests of, and shall make any order proper for the protection of, a bona fide purchaser or other person interested.

(4) A party or a person acting in collusion with a party may be ordered to pay the costs of any other party or of a bona fide purchaser or other person interested of and incidental to any such instrument or disposition and the setting aside or restraining of the instrument or disposition.

(4AA) An application may be made to the court for an order under this section by:
(a) a party to the proceedings; or
...

(4A) In addition to the powers the court has under this section, the court may also do any or all of the things listed in subsection 80(1) or 90SS(1).

...

(Emphasis added)
It is not in dispute that the share transfers effected by the husband to the third party in D Pty Ltd and E Pty Ltd are dispositions within the meaning of s 106B of the Act.
The husband concedes that he did not disclose the share transfers to the wife until 19 June 2023. The husband denies the allegation made by the wife that he intended to defeat her property claim. The husband’s Points of Defence plead, among other things, that he:
19. ...
t. ...
... did not consider the Businesses were part of the matrimonial asset pool because the [wife] made little to no contributions to their set-up, development and continuation;
... had no intention to deliberate [sic] conceal or remove assets or interests away from the [wife];
... has or had sufficient equity in the real properties of the matrimonial asset pool so that his interest would be capable of adjustment in favour of the [wife] for any deprivation of her interest in the Businesses ...
The evidence does not establish that the husband transferred the shares with the intention of defeating an anticipated order in the property settlement proceedings. However, the husband’s misapprehension as to the nature of property for the purposes of the wife’s s 79 property claim cannot act as a shield to resist the setting aside of relevant dispositions. As s 106B itself makes plain, dispositions may be set aside irrespective of intention.
It is common ground that the third party did not demand repayment of her loans until on or about 30 March 2023, and that her demand was made only after she was informed that the husband and wife had separated and that the wife had commenced property settlement proceedings. Further, it is not in dispute that the relevant dispositions occurred after this time, namely, in May and June 2023.
The third party could have demanded the transfer of shares in E Pty Ltd under the first loan agreement when it was not repaid by 9 September 2021, but she did not do so. The third party could have demanded the transfer of shares in D Pty Ltd under the second loan agreement when it was not repaid by 8 April 2022, but she did not do so. The third party did not demand the payment of interest under the loan agreements. It was only when she was given notice that the wife was seeking a property settlement from the husband and had commenced proceedings that she demanded the return of her loans and when that did not occur (other than for the sum of $160,000), she demanded the transfer of equity in the companies so that she could assume control of the [redacted] businesses.
The husband and third party argue that in circumstances where the value of the [redacted] businesses ‘roughly equated’ to the balance owing to the third party, the transfer of shares did not defeat an anticipated order for property settlement.
It is therefore necessary to consider the value of the “[redacted] businesses” operated by D Pty Ltd and E Pty Ltd and the balance of the debt then due to the third party.

The value of the “[redacted] businesses” and the debt in 2023

Value of the “[redacted] businesses”

The parties referred to the value of the husband’s interests in the companies, D Pty Ltd and E Pty Ltd, interchangeably with the value of the [redacted] businesses operated by them. The value of the [redacted] businesses was assessed by the single expert for the purpose of valuing the husband’s interests[2] in D Pty Ltd and E Pty Ltd. However, the value of the husband’s interests in the companies, of necessity, took into account related party loans. As the single expert explained - “if someone was to buy that business, they’re not going to take over someone’s other debts and related party loans”. What difference this might make to the argument relied upon by the husband and third party was not addressed.
The single expert was instructed to value the three [redacted] businesses operated by D Pty Ltd and E Pty Ltd. In the context of family law proceedings, the single expert valued the interests of the husband in the companies that operated the businesses and in so doing he took into account related party loans. This orthodox approach adopted by the single expert involved calculating the value of the [redacted] businesses using an earnings-based methodology and a net asset approach when valuing any surplus assets and/or liabilities of each company.
In his report filed on 22 December 2023 (“the 2023 report”), the single expert valued the husband’s interests in the companies at $974,500 as at the valuation date 31 March 2023.
There was an admitted arithmetical error in the 2023 report which he corrected in a supplementary report filed on 28 August 2024 (“the 2024 report”). The correction increased the value of the husband’s interests in the companies to $1,171,800. In the 2023 report, the single expert calculated the value of the [redacted] business operated by D Pty Ltd at $361,800, the value of the [redacted] businesses operated by E Pty Ltd at $197,300 for Business G and $562,700 for Business H. The total value of the three businesses was $1,121,800 using an earnings-based methodology. The value of the three businesses as opposed to the husband’s interests in the companies was not affected by the arithmetical error.
However, while not mentioned by any of the parties, the single expert did more than merely correct the arithmetical error, he updated his valuation at the valuation date 31 March 2023 by taking into account information that had become available after the valuation date.
The single expert “adopted the gross profit margins achieved by the respective businesses for the year ended 30 June 2024 as being more reflective of the future gross profit margins than that achieved in the year ended 30 June 2023”. This change increased the gross profit margin for D Pty Ltd from 59.53 per cent to 62.95 per cent (an adjustment of $99,430) and for E Pty Ltd from 59.66 per cent to 60.57 per cent for Business G (an adjustment of $26,733) and 57.32 per cent to 60.11 per cent for Business H (an adjustment of $86,534). The single expert also added back the closing stock for the business in City K ($27,500) and the closing stock for the business at Business G ($30,000) for the year ended 30 June 2023 and made adjustments for “repairs and maintenance” expenses for the year ended 30 June 2023 having regard to the “average” expenses for the years ended 30 June 2021 and 2022. This led to an adjustment of $23,197 for the [redacted] business operated by D Pty Ltd, $13,270 for the [redacted] business operated by E Pty Ltd at Shopping Centre EEE, and $8,792 for the [redacted] business operated by E Pty Ltd at Suburb V.
The adjusted figures were then used to calculate future maintainable earnings for each business. The single expert valued the [redacted] business operated by D Pty Ltd using the updated June 2023 figures at $699,500. The net surplus assets of D Pty Ltd were calculated at $403,700 which were added to the value of the business resulting in a valuation of the husband’s interest in D Pty Ltd at $1,103,200. The [redacted] business operated by E Pty Ltd at Shopping Centre EEE was valued at $354,800 and the [redacted] business at Suburb V at $777,200. The net surplus liabilities of E Pty Ltd were calculated at $552,800 which were deducted from the value of the businesses resulting in a valuation of the husband’s interest in E Pty Ltd at $579,200.

However, there is an additional factor relevant to the value of the businesses at the relevant time.
The wife’s brother, Mr GG, worked in the [redacted] business operated by D Pty Ltd for four years and seven months from January 2019 to August 2023, including two years and eight months as a full-time chef and at times as a manager. Mr GG contends that the husband instructed him to pay employees with cash that the husband had accumulated “without declaring” and Mr GG particularises this allegation with names, dates and amounts on various occasions during the period February 2023 to July 2023. Mr GG describes receiving instructions from the husband which involved only recording for tax purposes 15 per cent of the cash received and placing the balance in an envelope which was then used to pay staff in cash or the husband would collect it every two to three weeks. Mr GG deposes that the husband instructed the person in charge of closing the store to “mark the envelope with the cash amount for tax reporting purposes in parentheses and to indicate the cash amount that would not be reported for tax purposes”. Mr GG deposes that the “skimming activities began in early 2020 and occurred on a daily basis”. Mr GG demonstrated a sound knowledge of the procedures adopted by the husband in the operation of the D Pty Ltd business and some of the source documents upon which he relies to support his evidence are found in Exhibit 34. Mr GG’s evidence was not shaken during cross-examination and I accept his evidence.
Mr GG’s affidavit setting out the practice of under-declaring the cash receipts in the D Pty Ltd business was provided to the single expert. Notwithstanding the contested nature of Mr GG’s evidence, the single expert considered it to be important to have regard to the material provided by Mr GG to determine whether it would have an impact on his valuation assessment. The single expert opined, in the 2025 report, that unreported cash sales may have an impact. Further, he opined that unrecorded cash wages paid to some staff “would result in an understatement of labour costs and consequently, an overstatement of reported net profit”. The single export also noted that “unrecorded wages may indicate non-compliance with taxation, superannuation and employment law obligations”.
The single expert sets out his calculations of the possible impact of the cash receipts on the valuation of the business in 2023 in a very helpful table at paragraph 18 of the 2025 report, which uses as an example, the figures for May 2023. The table summarises the daily income, cash wages and cash disbursements in that month. The unrecorded cash, less cash business related outgoings, resulted in an adjusted profit figure of $17,155, ignoring any GST and tax implications.
It is also worth noting the single expert’s unchallenged evidence, that “typically the holiday periods such as December and January will be significantly busier than say the month [of] May” and that “on a straight-line annual extrapolation, this would result in adjusted profit (i.e. add back in [future maintainable earnings] calculations) of approximately $205,860 (i.e. $17,155 multiplied by 12)”.
Lastly, the single expert opines that the adjusted profit “would have a materially significant impact on the assessed Business Value as an EBITDA [earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortisation] multiple of 2.25 has been applied in the current matter” and he calculated that difference in the business value operated by D Pty Ltd as at 28 February 2025, if the unreported net cash were included, as being an additional $463,185. While the single expert was not asked to provide his opinion on what impact this “skimming” practice would have on the value of the D Pty Ltd business in 2023, I am of the view that it can safely be inferred that it would likely have had an impact. Further, it would be naïve to think that the husband only engaged in this “skimming” practice in the D Pty Ltd business.

Balance of debt

Regrettably, none of the parties provided a calculation for the balance of the three loans with interest as at the date of the share transfers. Further, notwithstanding the wife’s submission that interest on the loans should be calculated on a simple interest basis rather than compound, no calculations were provided on either basis. No submissions were made by the wife in support of the submission that simple rather than compound interest applied.
Counsel for the third party submitted that “[t]here’s nothing [in the loan agreements] that mentions compound interest, but on a close reading of that clause 2[.1(b)], I think it actually mentions compound interest. ... although it doesn’t say compound interest. That’s my interpretation, ... that it does actually mean compound interest”.
Contrary to the submissions made on behalf of the third party, the loan agreements specifically state at clause 3.1(b) that the borrower will pay “[i]nterest on the Monies, or such of the Monies as shall be unpaid, on each Interest Period, until repaid in full” and the definition of “Interest Period” states that it “means each month of the loan, or part thereof. Interest shall compound if not paid each Interest Period, and be added to the Monies” (emphasis added). I can see no basis to find that interest should be calculated on a basis other than compounding.

Conclusion

On any view, the husband and third party cannot maintain that the value of the businesses (or the value of the husband’s interests in the companies) as at 25 May 2023 and/or 5 June 2023 ‘roughly equated’ to the then balance of the loans owing to the third party.

Is there sufficient other property to achieve a just and equitable settlement without setting aside the share transfers?

In the alternative, the husband argues at paragraph 16 of his Case Outline that, even if the transfer of shares are found to defeat an anticipated claim, they should not be set aside because there is sufficient other property (taking into account the value of the businesses) to achieve a just and equitable property settlement without doing so. Regrettably, counsel for the husband did not address this alternative position during submissions.
The largely agreed balance sheet is set out later in these reasons. What it establishes is that without the value of the husband’s interests in the companies being included in the balance sheet, the net value of the property of the husband and wife or either of them is $2,183,092.63.
Doing the best I can in the circumstances to assess whether the husband’s alternative argument should be accepted, I will need to adopt the most recent value of the [redacted] businesses or the husband’s interests in the companies and then deduct the balance of the loans owing to the third party.
In the 2025 report, the single expert states that by joint letter dated 12 March 2025 he was requested to “provide an updated valuation of the following entities” namely, “[D Pty Ltd] trading as ‘[Business F in City K]’” and “[E Pty Ltd] [sic] trading as ‘[Business G in Shopping Centre EEE in City W]’ and ‘[Business H in Suburb V]’”.[3] The single expert states that he was requested to “include 2 valuation scenarios for the entities as follows:
a) A valuation incorporating the cash advances by the [third party]; and

b) A valuation which does not include any such loans.”
The single expert adopted 28 February 2025 as the valuation date. He relied upon the financial accounts being true and correct but as earlier noted, provided a significant caveat, namely, that “if there are unreported cash sales then this may impact on my valuation assessment” and if there were wages being paid in cash this “would result in an understatement of labour costs and consequently, an overstatement of reported net profit”.
It is also necessary to observe that, contrary to the position adopted at trial by the parties that the $160,000 repaid by the husband to the third party on 30 March 2023 should be treated as a reduction in principal of the first loan, the single expert proceeded on the basis that it was a payment of accrued interest and that the principal component of $650,000 ($200,000 to D Pty Ltd and $450,000 to E Pty Ltd) remained outstanding as at 28 February 2025. The parties did not challenge the single expert on his calculation of the liability owed to the third party, namely, $650,000.
For the purposes of this exercise, it seems to me to be appropriate to adopt the value of the husband’s interests in the companies as calculated by the single expert namely, $1,087,400 (scenario 1 - if the loan to the third party is included) or $1,737,400 (scenario 2 - if the loan to the third party is not included).
The net property of the parties or either of them would be $2,183,092.63 + $1,087,400 = $3,270,492.63 (with the loans deducted) or $2,183,092.63 + $1,737,400 = $3,920,492.63 (with the loans not deducted).
If the wife were to be awarded 40 per cent (which is the percentage the husband contends) of $3,270,492.63 she would be entitled to receive $1,308,197.05 or $1,568,197.05 of the higher figure.
If the wife were to be awarded 60 per cent (which is the percentage the wife contends) of $3,270,492.63 she would be entitled to receive $1,962,295.58, or $2,352,295.58 of the higher figure.
It is perhaps conceivable therefore that, if the share transfers are not set aside but the husband’s interests in D Pty Ltd and E Pty Ltd are included in the balance sheet to calculate the wife’s property settlement, the wife could receive a just and equitable settlement. However, in those circumstances the husband would not be reinstated as the owner of the companies and conceivably would receive very little or no property, given the concession on behalf of the husband, during submissions, that an award to the wife of 60 per cent would be open on the evidence. In those circumstances, I cannot be satisfied that it is possible to achieve a just and equitable settlement if the share transfers are not set aside but the value of the husband’s interests in the companies are included in the balance sheet.

SHOULD THE LOANS BE TREATED AS A LIABILITY OF THE HUSBAND ALONE AND NOT BROUGHT INTO ACCOUNT IN THE BALANCE SHEET?

The claim by the wife for certain dispositions/instruments to be set aside pursuant to s 106B of the Act was required to be formally pleaded, and the pleadings are before me. As earlier noted, the wife’s case substantially changed by the time of submissions. At that late stage, and seemingly without notice to the other parties, the wife abandoned her case to set aside the loan agreements, as she conceded that the loans were in fact made.
During submissions, the wife sought to argue a different case to that pleaded, namely, that the loans should be regarded as personal liabilities of the husband alone because he had obtained them in secret and failed to properly account for how he used the loans of $650,000 (other than for $45,000).
The wife’s submissions in support of her contention that the loans from the third party should be treated as a personal liability of the husband alone rather than debts of D Pty Ltd and E Pty Ltd may be summarised as follows:
(a) The third party and the husband treated the loans as though they were personal loans from the third party and the husband; and

(b) The husband has failed to corroborate what he did with the loans other than $45,000.

No objection was taken by counsel for the husband or counsel for the third party to the fact that the wife’s case, as it emerged during submissions, was not reflected in her Points of Claim.
Neither the husband nor the third party raised any issue of prejudice by the late change. Counsel for the husband, even conceded that in relation to the use made of the loans by the husband: “it would be better to have had everybody” to whom the husband paid money in cash from the loans received from the third party provide evidence, but submitted that the absence of corroboration from other potential suppliers who received cash from the husband for their services or supplies “goes to weight”.
Cross-examination of the husband and the third party established that they did not have a sophisticated understanding of the corporate structure surrounding their business operations and in simple terms regarded the loans as between themselves personally notwithstanding the loan agreements recording the parties to the loan agreements as the third party and the companies and that the husband was a guarantor. The cross-examination also established that the husband and the third party were not averse to retaining large sums of cash and paying suppliers and employees in cash.
The third party does not really have an interest in who pays the liability, as long as it is paid.
The third party submitted that the principal together with interest up to 1 December 2025 was $1,259,808.34. Neither the husband nor the wife addressed the Court on this calculation, and the calculation itself was not tendered.
In any event, during submissions, counsel for the third party properly conceded that the third party could not press for interest on the loans after 25 May 2023 as by then the third party had control of the companies which operated the [redacted] businesses and received the net profits. The single expert calculated the net profits before tax for D Pty Ltd for the financial year ended 30 June 2024 at $141,711 and for the eight months ended 28 February 2025 at $101,990. The net profits before tax for E Pty Ltd for the financial year ended 30 June 2024 were $218,467 and for the eight months ended 28 February 2025 the net profits were $223,791. The third party has continued to receive the net profits.

Conclusion

While I accept that the husband has failed to provide corroboration for the use of the $650,000 (other than $45,000), I do not consider the circumstances justify the husband alone being responsible for the entirety of the liability to the third party. As the cross-examination established, it was not unusual for the husband to deal in cash. It is common ground that fit outs were undertaken at the business premises, and that the husband continued to operate the [redacted] businesses after separation. Further, it is common ground that the husband set up a new business while the wife was overseas in 2022 which required a fit out. I consider a just and equitable outcome can be better achieved by taking the secretive nature of the loans and the absence of corroboration for the use of the balance of the loans into account when considering s 79(5)(e) of the Act.
The wife’s application for declarations that the loans were made to the husband personally rather than the companies fails.

APPLICABLE LEGAL PRINCIPLES – PROPERTY SETTLEMENT

In property settlement proceedings after the breakdown of a marriage, the Court may make such order as it considers appropriate, altering the interests of the parties to the marriage in the property of the parties or either of them, including an order for a settlement of property in substitution for any interest in the property for the benefit of the parties, and an order requiring either or both of the parties to the marriage to make, for the benefit of either or both of the parties or a child of the marriage, such settlement or transfer of property as the Court determines (s 79(1) of the Act).
The Court must not make an order unless it is satisfied that, in all the circumstances, it is just and equitable to make the order (s 79(2) of the Act).
Pursuant to s 79(3) of the Act, in considering what order (if any) should be made in property settlement proceedings, the Court:
(a) is to identify:
(i) the existing legal and equitable rights and interests in any property of the parties to the marriage or either of them; and

(ii) the existing liabilities of the parties to the marriage or either of them; and

(b) is to take into account ...:
(i) the considerations set out in subsection (4) (considerations relating to contributions); and

(ii) the considerations set out in subsection (5) (considerations relating to current and future circumstances).

The Court is required to take into account the considerations set out in s 79(4) of the Act, so far as relevant:
(a) the financial contribution made directly or indirectly by or on behalf of a party to the marriage, or a child of the marriage:
(i) to the acquisition, conservation or improvement of any of the property of the parties to the marriage or either of them; or
(ii) otherwise in relation to any of that last‑mentioned property;
whether or not that last‑mentioned property has, since the making of the contribution, ceased to be the property of the parties to the marriage or either of them;
(b) the contribution (other than a financial contribution) made directly or indirectly by or on behalf of a party to the marriage, or a child of the marriage:
(i) to the acquisition, conservation or improvement of any of the property of the parties to the marriage or either of them; or
(ii) otherwise in relation to any of that last‑mentioned property;
whether or not that last‑mentioned property has, since the making of the contribution, ceased to be the property of the parties to the marriage or either of them;
(c) the contribution made by a party to the marriage to the welfare of the family constituted by the parties to the marriage and any children of the marriage, including any contribution made in the capacity of homemaker or parent;
(ca) the effect of any family violence, to which one party to the marriage has subjected or exposed the other party, on the ability of a party to the marriage to make the kind of contributions referred to in paragraphs (a), (b) and (c);

(d) the effect of any proposed order upon the earning capacity of either party to the marriage;

(f) any other order made under this Act affecting a party to the marriage or a child of the marriage;

(g) any child support under the Child Support (Assessment) Act 1989 that a party to the marriage has provided for a child of the marriage.
The Court is required to take into account the considerations set out in s 79(5) of the Act, so far as relevant:
(a) the effect of any family violence, to which one party to the marriage (the subject marriage) has subjected or exposed the other party, on the current and future circumstances of the other party, including on any of the matters mentioned elsewhere in this subsection;
(b) the age and state of health of each of the parties to the subject marriage;

(c) the income, property and financial resources of each of the parties to the subject marriage and the physical and mental capacity of each of them for appropriate gainful employment;

(d) the effect of any material wastage, caused intentionally or recklessly by a party to the subject marriage, of property or financial resources of either of the parties to the subject marriage or both of them;

(e) any liabilities incurred by either of the parties to the subject marriage or both of them, including the nature of the liabilities and the circumstances relating to them;

(f) the extent to which either party to the subject marriage has the care of a child of the marriage who has not attained the age of 18 years, including the need of either party to provide appropriate housing for such a child;

(g) commitments of each of the parties to the subject marriage that are necessary to enable the party to support themselves and any child or other person that the party has a duty to maintain;

(h) the responsibilities of either party to the subject marriage to support any other person;

(i) the eligibility of either party to the subject marriage for a pension, allowance or benefit under:
(i) any law of the Commonwealth, of a State or Territory or of another country; or

(ii) any superannuation fund or scheme, whether the fund or scheme was established, or operates, within or outside Australia;
(j) if either party to the subject marriage is eligible for a pension, allowance or benefit as mentioned in paragraph (i)—the rate at which it is being paid to the party;

(k) a standard of living that in all the circumstances is reasonable;

(l) the extent to which an alteration of the interests of the parties to the subject marriage in any property would enable a party to undertake education or establish a business or otherwise obtain an adequate income;

(m) the effect of any proposed order on the ability of a creditor of a party to the subject marriage to recover the creditor’s debt, so far as that effect is relevant;

(n) the extent to which each party to the subject marriage has contributed to the income, earning capacity, property and financial resources of the other party;

(o) the duration of the subject marriage and the extent to which it has affected the earning capacity of each party to the subject marriage;

(p) the need to protect a party to the marriage who wishes to continue that party’s role as a parent;

(q) if either party to the subject marriage is cohabiting with another person—the financial circumstances relating to the cohabitation;

(r) the terms of any order or declaration made, or proposed to be made, under this Part in relation to:
(i) a party to the subject marriage (in relation to another marriage); or

(ii) a person who is a party to another marriage with a party to the subject marriage; or

(iii) the property of a person covered by subparagraph (i) and of a person covered by subparagraph (ii), or of either of them; or

(iv) vested bankruptcy property in relation to a person covered by subparagraph (i) or (ii);
(s) the terms of any order or declaration made, or proposed to be made, under Part VIII in relation to:
(i) a party to the subject marriage; or

(ii) a person who is party to a marriage with a party to the subject marriage; or

(iii) the property of a person covered by subparagraph (i) and of a person covered by subparagraph (ii), or of either of them; or

(iv) vested bankruptcy property in relation to a person covered by subparagraph (i) or (ii);
(t) any child support under the Child Support (Assessment) Act 1989 that a party to the subject marriage is to provide, or might be liable to provide in the future, for a child of the subject marriage;

(u) the terms of any Part VIIIAB financial agreement that is binding on either or both of the parties to the subject marriage;

(v) the terms of any financial agreement that is binding on a party to the subject marriage;

(w) any other fact or circumstance which, in the opinion of the court, the justice of the case requires to be taken into account.

(Emphasis in original)
The discretion as to whether or not to make a property settlement order, although extraordinarily wide, must nevertheless be exercised in a principled way.[4]There is no presumption that the parties’ rights to or interests in property are or should be different from those that currently exist.[5] The consideration of whether it is just and equitable to make an order should not be considered by reference only to the matters in s 79(4) of the Act. It is necessary to give separate consideration to ss 79(2) and (4) of the Act and not to ‘conflate’ the two subsections.[6]

IS IT JUST AND EQUITABLE TO MAKE A PROPERTY ORDER?

I am satisfied that it is just and equitable to make a property settlement order in circumstances where the common use of property ceased upon separation and both the husband and the wife seek that an order be made.

The footnotes to Exhibit 37 are as follows:

CONSIDERATIONS RELATING TO CONTRIBUTIONS – S 79(4)

The wife submits that when taking into account the considerations relating to contributions, they should be assessed as equal (although that is on the basis that the loans are treated not as a liability of the companies but as a debt solely of the husband and not included in the balance sheet).
Counsel for the wife submits that “while the husband was building up his business assets ... the wife after the birth of the first child, ... devoted herself to raising a family and caring for them”. Counsel for the wife eschews the notion of contributions being assessed as other than equal.
The husband submits that contributions should be assessed as favouring him in the proportion 60/40 because of his greater financial contribution, establishing and operating the [redacted] businesses, purchasing the real properties, and constructing homes on two of the properties.
The husband contends that he had $140,000 in savings at the commencement of the relationship. The wife disputes the husband’s assertion and there is no evidence to support it.
It is common ground that the husband played a greater role in the establishment and operation of the [redacted] businesses and to the acquisition and improvement of the real properties. However, it is also common ground that the wife played a greater homemaker and parenting role. While the husband was playing a greater role in operating the businesses, the wife was caring for two young children. It will be recalled that the husband and wife lived in different cities from September 2019 until July 2020 and in different countries from January 2022 until December 2022. The children remained with the wife during these periods.
The wife was a joint borrower for the acquisition of the Suburb FF property, and the husband borrowed money from D Pty Ltd and/or E Pty Ltd to assist in the acquisition or improvement to some of the real properties.
Counsel for the husband concedes that “[b]ecause there was a domestic violence factor, the mother’s contribution may be higher” and that it would be open to find that “contributions” should be assessed as favouring the wife in the proportion 60/40. While eschewing that current or future factors should result in an adjustment in favour of the wife, counsel for the husband concedes that an overall division 60/40 in the wife’s favour was open by increasing her contribution assessment and/or making an adjustment in her favour for unspecified current or future factors pursuant to s 79(5) of the Act and that “both ranges would be available to your Honour as a reasonable outcome”.

Family Violence

It is common ground that findings made by a Magistrate after a contested domestic violence hearing between the husband and the wife on [redacted] 2023 are binding on the husband and wife in these proceedings. A transcript of reasons for judgment delivered by the Magistrate was tendered in the current proceedings by consent (Exhibit 6).
During the domestic violence proceedings, the following concessions were made by counsel representing the husband:
(a) That the husband committed financial abuse towards the wife after separation by withdrawing $122,000 from a joint bank account, being $120,000 on 6 January 2023 and the balance withdrawn by 8 March 2023;

(b) That the husband committed financial abuse towards the wife after separation by withdrawing $341,000 from a joint bank account on 8 March 2023; and

(c) That the husband committed financial abuse towards the wife by failing to pay spouse maintenance pursuant to a court order.

The husband conceded during cross-examination in the domestic violence proceedings that he had not told the wife what became of the funds he withdrew. No submissions were made in the current proceedings, about the funds referred to by the Magistrate. However, as part of Exhibit 35 (amended index and annexures to the husband’s trial affidavit), the husband provides an account of the movement of the $120,500 withdrawn by him on 6 January 2023 between various accounts. The funds ended up as part of the loan to Mr RR which is included as the husband’s asset in the balance sheet. I was not taken to any evidence relating to the $341,000 and I have been unable to locate any evidence relating thereto.
The following findings were made by the Magistrate:
(a) The husband committed three acts of physical domestic violence against the wife by choking her on 31 December 2016, 22 July 2019 and 13 November 2021;

(b) These were severe acts of domestic violence;

(c) The third act of choking occurred in the presence of the children;

(d) The husband did not commit domestic violence by way of financial abuse prior to separation;

(e) During a telephone call between the husband and the wife on 4 January 2023, the husband made “overt threats against the [wife’s] financial security as well as emotional abuse, direct abuse and attempted manipulation” amounting to an attempt “to coerce or bully [the wife] into accepting a financial settlement on terms far less favourable than she might otherwise accept or receive”;

(f) The husband did not accept “at that point that he ha[d] any obligations to support [the wife] or the children” and “then promptly [took] actions, just as he foreshadowed, to remove significant funds from the joint bank accounts which, it seems, the [wife] was not fully aware that she could access”.

It will be recalled that s 79(4)(ca) of the Act provides that the Court is to take into account, so far as relevant:
the effect of any family violence, to which one party to the marriage has subjected or exposed the other party, on the ability of a party to the marriage to make the kind of contributions referred to in paragraphs (a), (b) and (c);
Given the serious nature of the family violence perpetrated by the husband against the wife during the marriage, and the concession made by the husband’s counsel of its relevance when assessing contributions, it seems to me that an inference can be drawn that the family violence had an adverse effect on the mother’s ability to make contributions.[7] One such effect was that after one of the choking incidents the mother moved to Brisbane with the children. The wife’s ability to make contributions, other than as a parent, would thereby have been adversely affected.

Conclusion – contributions

The Court is required to adopt a “holistic approach” when assessing the “myriad of contributions”[8] made by each of the husband and the wife from the commencement of their relationship including the likely adverse effect on the wife’s ability to make contributions by reason of family violence perpetrated upon her by the husband during their marriage. I conclude that contributions should be assessed as favouring the wife in the proportion 52.5/47.5.

CONSIDERATIONS RELATING TO CURRENT AND FUTURE CIRCUMSTANCES

Counsel for the wife submits that a just and equitable outcome would result in “an ultimate split of 60/40 in [the wife’s] favour based on family violence, future needs where she has got predominant care of the children ... [and] her earnings are very low”. No submissions were made on the appropriate percentage distribution if the loan balance was included in the balance sheet as opposed to being the husband’s liability alone.
The wife submits that considerations that are relevant to current and future circumstances which favour an adjustment in her favour such that overall, the property is adjusted in the proportion 60/40 in her favour are as follows:
(a) The effect of family violence to which the wife was subjected, on her current and future circumstances (s 79(5)(a)); and

(b) The greater extent to which the wife has the care of the two children who have not attained the age of 18 years, including the need to provide appropriate housing for the children (s 79(5)(f)).

As already indicated, I also propose to take into account the liability incurred by the husband to the third party as a relevant consideration under s 79(5)(e) of the Act.
The wife’s submissions did not address the effect of family violence on her current and future circumstances. However, it was conceded on behalf of the husband that the husband’s financial abuse of the wife, perpetrated after separation, as found by the Magistrate, which included the failure to pay spouse maintenance as ordered and withdrawing significant sums from a joint account, were factors that could be considered under s 79(5)(a).
The wife is currently working as a child-care educator and also studying nursing. Her current weekly income is $1,932.15, comprising $400 in wages, $400 in parenting payment, $147.70 in Family Tax Benefit, $84.45 in child support and $900 in spouse maintenance. As earlier noted, the wife does not seek an order for ongoing spouse maintenance.
The husband is a chef although currently unemployed. There is no suggestion that the husband will remain unemployed after the finalisation of these proceedings. It was conceded, on the husband’s behalf, that his earning capacity is “significant” when compared with the wife. The husband has been receiving the rental income of $2,667.50 per week from the investment properties. Currently, the husband has a surplus of income over expenses of $927.65 per week and after the proceedings are finalised his obligation to pay $900 spouse maintenance will cease. Both the husband and the wife seek the sale of the investment properties so the husband will cease to receive rental income.
The husband obtained $650,000 in loans from the third party unbeknown to the wife. He admits he kept the existence of the loans secret from the wife. It is common ground that he has repaid $160,000 of the principal but no interest. Doing the best I can on the evidence before me and in the absence of assistance from the parties, I have calculated that the debt owing to the third party up to 25 May 2023 was $797,179.78 and that no further interest should be paid on the loans after that date because the third party retained the net profit from the [redacted] businesses.
Although the husband failed to corroborate the entire use of the cash loans it was established during cross-examination that the husband regularly paid staff and suppliers in cash. Accordingly, while it was in the husband’s control to provide evidence corroborating his use of the cash and he failed to do so, I consider it likely that a significant portion of the loans were used as stated by him. The fact that he obtained the loans in secret and that he failed to fully corroborate his expenditure of the loans are considerations that I take into account when making an adjustment in the wife’s favour.

Conclusion – s79(5) factors

I conclude that an adjustment should be made in favour of the wife of 12.5 per cent after considering the relevant s 79(5) factors, which would result in an overall percentage distribution in the wife’s favour of 65/35.

REFERRAL OF PAPERS TO THE ATTORNEY-GENERAL’S DEPARTMENT

It became apparent during the trial that numerous offences under various Commonwealth and State laws may have been committed by the husband, and the wife, and the third party in the conduct of the [redacted] businesses and other matters.
In relation to possible tax evasion, I have accepted evidence from Mr GG that the husband instructed staff not to record all cash receipts at the [redacted] business operated by D Pty Ltd and that cash was put in a work safe and collected by the husband and either used to pay suppliers in cash or kept in a safe in his home. The single expert analysed the evidence for just one month, namely, May 2023, and noted unreported cash of $20,471.59. The single expert further noted that it would be expected that “typically the holiday periods such as December and January will be significantly busier than say the month [of] May”. The single expert opined that his value of the [redacted] business operated by D Pty Ltd would likely increase by $463,185 if the unreported cash were included. It is perhaps likely that the husband’s practice of not recording cash receipts was also a feature of the [redacted] businesses conducted by E Pty Ltd.
In relation to possible offences of fraud, the husband admitted that on 6 June 2023 he had knowingly purchased a property (in his own name) on behalf of a foreign national, Mr RR, and in so doing the Foreign Acquisitions and Takeovers Act 1975 (Cth) and its associated regulations may have been breached.
During cross-examination, the husband admitted to entering into oral contracts with the following subcontractors, contrary to the Queensland Building and Construction Commission Act 1991 (Qld):
(a) Mr HH, tiler, for $45,000;

(b) Business UU for $35,000;

(c) Business VV for shutter and auto-door installation for $35,000;

(d) Business WW for shelf and stainless steel structure works for $40,000.

In relation to possible breaches of visa conditions, the husband admits paying cash to students on restricted work visas who had exceeded their permitted hours of work. The wife also concedes in her trial affidavit that she was aware of certain visa conditions restricting her rights relating to the operation of further businesses which were circumvented by the husband being the director and shareholder of E Pty Ltd. The husband deposes in his trial affidavit that the wife was required under her visa conditions to remain and work full time in regional Queensland and that she breached these conditions when she moved to Brisbane in September 2019.
The husband admitted that D Pty Ltd and E Pty Ltd were liable to pay payroll tax. He further admitted that he failed to record cash payments to students thus possibly committing offences under the Payroll Tax Act 1971 (Qld) and offences under the Income Tax Assessment Act 1936 (Cth) and the Income Tax Assessment Act 1997 (Cth) (collectively “the Taxation Acts”).
The husband admitted that the loans he received from the third party on behalf of D Pty Ltd and E Pty Ltd were not recorded in his company records nor deposited into company accounts. This may be in breach of his obligation to keep proper books and records as required by the Corporations Act 2001 (Cth). I do not accept the husband’s evidence that he told his accountant at the relevant time, Mr HHH, of the loans.
The third party admitted that she had no reason to doubt information provided by the Department of Home Affairs that she flew out of Australia on flight [redacted] on 9 July 2023 and did not return until 2 September 2023 on flight [redacted] (Exhibit 19). Exhibits 17 (2023 Activity Statement Substantiation Declaration for D Pty Ltd) and 18 (2023 Activity Statement Substantiation Declaration for E Pty Ltd) were signed by the third party purportedly at her accountant’s office in Australia on 23 August 2023. The third party conceded that she “signed it perhaps a different day”. The third party’s evidence indicates the possibility of fraud.
The third party admitted during cross-examination that she “didn’t report the cash income to the government” indicating possible offences under the Taxation Acts.
The wife deposes in her trial affidavit that the husband “created payslips to make it appear as though [she] worked at the [businesses], allocating his income to [her] in order to reduce his tax burden” and that in the financial years 2022 and 2023 when her tax returns recorded her taxable income as $89,919 and $76,379 respectively, she did not work in the businesses at all and was not even living in City K or City W where the businesses were located. The wife deposes that she started casual employment at a child care centre on 13 February 2023. This evidence may indicate that the husband and the wife have committed offences under the Taxation Acts.
The husband deposes in his trial affidavit that the “main purpose of arranging the salary payments to [the wife] in FY2022 and FY2023 was not to avoid or reduce my income tax burden ... but rather to support the application for the home loan and construction loan for [the] [Suburb FF] Property”. This may leave the husband and wife open to possible prosecution for fraud under s 408C of the Criminal Code 1899 (Qld).
As I am satisfied of the possible commission of various offences I am obligated to refer the papers,[9] and as the types of offences are broad ranging, it seems to me to be appropriate to refer the papers to the Attorney-General’s Department so that consideration might be given to which departments and authorities should be alerted to possible offences and other breaches of the law so that the relevant papers can be directed to those departments and/or authorities.
I propose to direct that the Principal Registrar of this Court provide the following documents to the Attorney-General’s Department for consideration of what possible offences have been committed and to direct relevant court papers to the relevant departments and/or authorities:
