REFERRAL OF PRACTITIONERS TO LEGAL SERVICES COMMISSION

The third party made several admissions during her evidence that may tend to prove that she has committed offences under Australian law. For example, the third party agreed that she did not report the cash income received in her business “to the government”. Her counsel did not seek that her client be given a warning when the topic of failing to disclose cash income was first canvassed during cross-examination nor seek that her client be given a certificate in respect of self-incrimination pursuant to s 128 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) (“Evidence Act”). Eventually, an application was made but only after the incriminating evidence was already given and the witness was no longer compelled to give the evidence.
During her submissions, counsel for the third party, said the following:
... Now, there was something said yesterday afternoon which was would appear to be an admission by the [third party] that she had not been telling the ATO about all the tax. Your Honour, that was so vague with absolutely no amounts that nobody could place any reliance or interpret anything against the [third party] on that particular statement that she made, ...
When given the opportunity to make submissions about whether a referral to the Legal Services Commission should not be made, the following exchange occurred:
HER HONOUR: Yes. Now, your client gave some evidence that was potentially incriminating, and no direction was sought by you. Should I consider making a referral to Legal Services Commission for that failure to seek a direction?

COUNSEL FOR THE

THIRD PARTY: Well, your Honour, maybe yes, maybe no, but, your Honour, I didn’t ask for a section 128 certificate because – I can’t talk about why, but I was surprised, your Honour, and in any event, the concession that was made was so vague and so indeterminate that, your Honour, I don’t think it is likely to be taken seriously by any other authority, but, your Honour, one can only go on one’s instructions, and as I say, the concession was so vague that one couldn’t place any great reliance on it in any event.
Any referral I make will not of course determine whether counsel for the third party has engaged in unsatisfactory professional conduct or professional misconduct. That will be a matter for the Legal Services Commissioner in New South Wales.
The husband also made a number of admissions during his evidence that may tend to prove that he has committed offences under Australian law. For example, that he had knowingly participated in purchasing real property in Australia for a foreign national because the foreign national was not permitted to do so under Australian law. His counsel did not seek that his client be given a warning at any time nor seek that his client be given a certificate in respect of self-incrimination pursuant to s 128 of the Evidence Act. It was apparent from a proposed application raised on the first day of the trial that it was anticipated that the husband may give evidence on particular matters that may tend to prove that he had committed an offence under Australian law.
During his submissions, counsel for the husband said:
... the answers given by [the husband] do not directly implicate him on any specific instances of actions that attract liability. For example, cash wages to staff. We don’t know who they are. We don’t know the amounts they were paid. We don’t know anything about the circumstances surrounding their pay. It would be essentially another unnecessary investigation based upon speculation about [the husband’s] potential to have done something wrong without any real knowledge of the basis of that potential, so in that sense, two things are true. He hasn’t incriminated himself, and second of all, perhaps a reasonable restraint would be satisfactory in these circumstances to not issue any Legal Services Commission complaints.
I intend to refer counsel for the husband to the Legal Services Commission, Queensland. It will be a matter for the Commissioner whether the conduct warrants investigation.
While the wife also gave potentially incriminating evidence, she did so in her evidence in chief and, as I am entitled to assume that her solicitor, as an officer of the Court, gave the wife appropriate advice about the consequences of doing so, I do not intend to refer her solicitor in relation to this matter. No doubt, if the wife did not receive such advice she will make her own complaint to the Legal Services Commission, Queensland.
However, I do have a concern that the wife’s solicitor may have engaged in unnecessary work at significant cost for the wife.
The trial directions made on 10 March 2025 included the following:
In the event that a party refers to a document within the body of an affidavit of evidence in chief, any such document is not to be annexed to the said affidavit, unless it is a report by a single expert or treating practitioner, but is to be included in an indexed and paginated folder (including a reference to the paragraph number within any affidavit where the document is mentioned) and provided to the other parties at the time of service of the affidavit AND a copy of the folder should be available to be tendered, if required, by forwarding an electronic copy of the bundle, which is to be paginated to reflect the electronic page numbering, to Associate.JusticeCarew@fcfcoa.gov.au prior to the commencement of trial.
...
After inspection of documents produced to this Honourable Court pursuant to subpoena, the applicant and respondents each provide to the other a list of any documents requested by them to be included in a bundle of documents to be tendered at trial by consent no later than 4.00pm on 20 October 2025. The applicant prepare and circulate to the other parties an index of the documents to be included in the tender bundle by no later than 4.00pm on 3 November 2025 and at trial, have available to tender, the bundle indexed and paginated, with the page numbering to reflect the electronic page numbering, by emailing a copy of the electronic bundle prior to the commencement of trial to Associate.JusticeCarew@fcfcoa.gov.au.
A “trial bundle” was sent to my Chambers by the wife’s solicitors on 21 November 2025 comprising 3742 pages. Totally unnecessarily the bundle included all material filed in the Court comprising 3686 pages.
A “supplementary trial bundle” was sent to my Chambers by the wife’s solicitors on 28 November 2025 comprising 1,252 pages.
On 2 December 2025, an “amended supplementary trial bundle” was sent to my Chambers by the wife’s solicitors comprising 1,545 pages. It was not apparent on the face of that bundle what the amendments were.
A further agreed “trial bundle” was sent to my Chambers by the wife’s solicitors on 5 December 2025 comprising 2,148 pages.
My concern is that the wife’s solicitors may have charged for the photocopying of the almost entirely unnecessary tender bundles. The photocopying charge under the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) sch 3 item 107 is $1 per page. It will be recalled that the wife’s costs in these proceedings have been estimated at an astronomical $912,000 at least.
I propose to refer the wife’s solicitors to the Legal Services Commission, Queensland. It will be a matter for the Commissioner whether the conduct warrants investigation.
The trial directions made on 10 March 2025 made clear that any proposed tender bundle was to include documents produced pursuant to subpoena not documents filed in the court. Any bundle was also to be indexed and paginated to reflect the electronic pages.
As my referral of counsel and solicitors is not a matter about which I make any findings of professional misconduct or unsatisfactory professional conduct, their names in the order and in the reasons will be redacted from the online and other publication of the reasons.
